Practical 1: Creating Interactive Visualisations Using Tableau¶

Information Details
Module ID CS5044
Author 250025167

I. Data Exploration¶

Before conducting the data familiarisation, this section summarises the key information provided in the documentation linked to the practical PDF (CPJ Doc: https://cpj.org/data/methodology/).

A) Analysis of the documentation¶

1 - Source of the database¶

The data is collected from a wide variety of primary and secondary sources to ensure global coverage. CPJ researchers collect information from:

  • Local and international media reports.
  • Human rights organizations and journalists’ unions.
  • Direct testimony from local correspondents, family members, or colleagues of the victims.

2 - Timeline¶

The database tracks incidents spanning from 1992 to the present (as per documentation).

3 - Data collection methodology¶

The collection process is manual:

  • Every case is independently verified by CPJ researchers.
  • Information is cross-checked across multiple sources to ensure accuracy.
  • The CPJ only classifies a death as confirmed if they can affirm with reasonable certainty that the journalist was killed because of their work.

4 - Key Variables¶

To understand the dataset, several key definitions and classifications are used:

  • Definition of Journalist: Includes anyone who regularly covers news or comments on public affairs through any medium to share fact-based information.
  • Classification: Deaths are categorized as Confirmed (motive confirmed) or Unconfirmed (motive is not yet proven, though the journalist was killed).
  • Media Workers: Note that media workers (e.g., drivers, translators) are usually tracked separately from the main journalist category in separate datasets.

5 - Constraints of the database¶

While comprehensive, the database has specific limitations:

  • Manual Updates: Since the database is updated manually when new information surfaces, there can be a slight lag in real-time reporting.
  • Verification Barriers: If information is insufficient or impossible to verify, a case may remain unconfirmed indefinitely.
  • Data Labeling: Because data comes from diverse global sources, inconsistencies or errors in labeling can occur.
  • Intentional Gaps: Specific data points may be listed as unknown (such as place of death) intentionally when information cannot be verified, rather than being a mistake.

B) Basic Analysis¶

In [ ]:
# Import pandas and numpy libraries
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
In [ ]:
# Load the CSV file into a dataframe
df = pd.read_csv("raw data/Practical1_Journalists_Killed_Imprisoned_1992_2020.csv")
pd.set_option("display.max_colwidth", None)

# Standardize missing values by replacing common placeholders with NaN
missing_like = ["", " ", "NA", "N/A", "null", "NULL", "None", "NONE", "Unknown", "unknown"]
df = df.replace(missing_like, np.nan)

# Remove leading/trailing spaces and normalize multiple spaces in text columns
df = df.apply(lambda x: x.str.replace(r"\s+", " ", regex=True).str.strip() if x.dtype == "object" else x)
In [ ]:
# Display basic information about the dataset
print("Shape:", df.shape)

print("------------------------------------------------")
print("Random Sample of the Dataset:")
print("------------------------------------------------")
display(df.sample(5, random_state=100))

print("------------------------------------------------")
print("Data Types by Column:")
print("------------------------------------------------")
display(df.dtypes.sort_values())

print("------------------------------------------------")
print("Descriptive Statistics and Dataset Summary:")
print("------------------------------------------------")
display(df.describe(include="all").T)

In [ ]:
# Create a summary table describing key characteristics of each column:
# - number of unique values
# - percentage of unique values
# - proportion of missing values
#
# This overview helps identify:
# - high-cardinality variables
# - potential data quality issues
# - columns that may not be suitable for visualization

u_table = pd.DataFrame({"n_unique": df.nunique(dropna=True)})
u_table["ttl_size"] = len(df)
u_table["unique_%"] = (u_table["n_unique"] / len(df) * 100).round(2)
u_table["NaN_%"] = (df.isna().mean() * 100).round(2)

print("------------------------------------------------")
print("Column Summary: Unique Values and Missing Data")
print("------------------------------------------------")
display(u_table.sort_values("n_unique", ascending=False))

# Since most columns in the dataframe are of type "object",
# we examine the distribution of values for each categorical column
cat_cols = df.select_dtypes(include="object").columns

# Display the most frequent values in each categorical column
for col in cat_cols:
    counts = df[col].value_counts()
    duplicates = counts[counts > 1]

    if not duplicates.empty:
        print("------------------------------------------------")
        print("Column:", col)
        print("------------------------------------------------")
        print(duplicates.head(10))

In [ ]:
# Temporarily normalize text to detect inconsistent labels
# (e.g. differences in capitalization or spacing)
def normalize_text(series):
    return (
        series.astype("string")
        .str.strip()
        .str.lower()
        .str.replace(r"\s+", " ", regex=True)
    )

# Identify categorical values that differ only by formatting
# by comparing original values with their normalized version
def find_case_variants(df, col):
    temp = pd.DataFrame({"original": df[col].dropna(), "normalized": normalize_text(df[col].dropna())})
    grouped = (temp.groupby("normalized")["original"].unique().reset_index())

    grouped["n_variants"] = grouped["original"].apply(len)
    variants = grouped[grouped["n_variants"] > 1]
    return variants.sort_values("n_variants", ascending=False)
In [ ]:
# Detect inconsistent labels in categorical columns (like differences in typo, capitale letter or spacing).
for i, col in enumerate(cat_cols, start=1):
    variants = find_case_variants(df, col)

    if not variants.empty:
        print("------------------------------------------------")
        print(f"Column n°{i}: {col}")
        print("------------------------------------------------")

        display(variants)

102 not sentenced [Not Sentenced, Not sentenced] 2

Now, we also want to examine whether different columns contain overlapping values. The goal is not only to identify similarities between variables, but also to detect possible data quality issues, such as values that may have been entered in the wrong column.

To do so, we compute a similarity matrix between categorical columns using the Jaccard similarity coefficient:

$$Similarity = \frac{|A \cap B|}{|A \cup B|}$$

For each pair of columns, we compare their sets of unique values. A high similarity score means that the two columns share many values. This can reveal:

  1. redundancy between columns
  2. conceptual overlap
  3. or values that may be misplaced

After identifying suspicious column pairs, we can list the shared values and inspect them manually in order to confirm whether they are valid or incorrect. For example, if two semantically different columns share the same values, those shared elements can be extracted and reviewed individually to determine whether they correspond to valid overlap or to a placement error.

In [ ]:
# Comparison of element between to of the same colunm, by using Jaccard similarity
# Import seaborn and matplotlibs libraries
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.colors import LogNorm

columns = df.select_dtypes(include="object").columns
matrix = pd.DataFrame(index=columns, columns=columns, dtype=float)

# Compute pairwise Jaccard similarity between categorical columns
for col1 in columns:
    # Create a set of unique non-null values for the first column
    set1 = set(df[col1].dropna().astype(str))

    for col2 in columns:
        # Create a set of unique non-null values for the second column
        set2 = set(df[col2].dropna().astype(str))

        # Compute intersection and union of both sets
        intersection = len(set1 & set2)
        union = len(set1 | set2)

        # Calculate Jaccard similarity score
        if union == 0:
            score = 0
        else:
            score = intersection / union

        # Store the similarity score in the matrix
        matrix.loc[col1, col2] = score
In [ ]:
# Visualize the similarity matrix using a heatmap
plt.figure(figsize=(24,10))

# Hide cells with zero similarity to improve readability
mask = (matrix == 0)
sns.heatmap(
    matrix,
    mask=mask,
    cmap="YlOrBr",
    annot=True,
    fmt=".3%",
    vmax=0.1,
    norm=LogNorm(vmin=0.0001, vmax=1),
    linewidths=1,
    cbar=False
)

plt.title("Column Similarity Matrix", fontsize=16)
plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha="right")
plt.yticks(rotation=0)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [ ]:

C) Conclusion¶

The analysis identified several data quality issues:

  1. Large proportions of missing values in multiple columns.

  2. Redundant geographical attributes containing overlapping information.

  3. Inconsistent formatting in categorical variables (capitalisation and spelling).

  4. Multiple values stored within a single cell in several attributes.

  5. Encoding errors in some text fields.

  6. Geographic data stored at different hierarchical levels within the same column.

  7. Some columns containing no useful analytical information.

Therefore, a structured data-cleaning process is required to standardise textual values, reorganise the geographic hierarchy, and remove redundant or unusable attributes before building the visualisations.

II. Data Cleaning & Preparation¶

As we saw in the previous section, the dataset is far from being usable in its current state. Therefore, we need to clean and prepare the data in order to extract as much relevant information as possible.

The dataset is collected by the Committee to Protect Journalists through investigative research based on multiple sources. As a result, some variables contain missing information, multiple categories, or inconsistent formatting originating from the original reports.

I will then clean the database in 6 step :

a. Standardisation of categorical labels
b. Correction of inconsistencies across columns
c. Handling of multi-value categorical fields
d. Removal of redundant or unused variables
e. Creation of derived relational tables

For the organisation it's not a problem, since the name of the organisation generali has a country name, or a longer name, so we will not change this columns.

A) Standardisation of categorical labels¶

In [ ]:
# This dataframe will allow us to applie and test the modification.
df_clean = df.copy()
In [ ]:
df_clean = df_clean.sort_values(by=['fullName', 'year'], ascending=[True, True])
In [ ]:
employedAs_corrections = {
    "staff": "Staff"
}

organizations_corrections = {
    "dicle news agency (diha)": "Dicle News Agency (DİHA)",
    "dicle news agency (di̇ha)": "Dicle News Agency (DİHA)",
    "el-shaab el-jadeed": "El-Shaab el-Jadeed",
    "former journalist": "Former Journalist",
    "freelance": "Freelance"
}

location_corrections = {
    "deir al-zour": "Deir al-Zour",
    "place of death unknown": "Place of Death Unknown"
}

length_sentence_corrections = {
    "not sentenced": "Not Sentenced",
    "0-5 years": "0-5 Years",
    "5-10 years": "5-10 Years",
    "10+ years": "10+ Years"
}

sentence_corrections = {
    "1 year": "1 Year",
    "5 years": "5 Years",
    "7 years": "7 Years",
    "12 years": "12 Years",
    "not sentenced": "Not Sentenced"
}

jobs_corrections = {
    "internet reporter": "Internet Reporter",
    "print reporter": "Print Reporter",
    "editor": "Editor",
    "broadcast reporter": "Broadcast Reporter",
    "photographer": "Photographer",
    "producer": "Producer",
    "publisher/owner": "Publisher/Owner",
    "columnist/commentator": "Columnist/Commentator",
    "camera operator": "Camera Operator",
    "documentary filmmaker": "Documentary Filmmaker"
}

coverage_corrections = {
    "human rights": "Human Rights",
    "politics": "Politics",
    "corruption": "Corruption",
    "crime": "Crime",
    "culture": "Culture",
    "war": "War",
    "sports": "Sports"
}

mediums_corrections = {
    "print": "Print"
}

charges_corrections = {
    "anti-state": "Anti-State",
    "false news": "False News",
    "retaliatory": "Retaliatory",
    "censorship violation": "Censorship Violation",
    "defamation": "Defamation",
    "ethnic or religious insult": "Ethnic or Religious Insult",
    "no charge": "No Charge"
}
In [ ]:
# For columns containing a single value per cell
def normalize_single_value(series, corrections):
    return series.apply(
        lambda x: corrections.get(str(x).strip().lower(), x) if pd.notna(x) else x
    )

# For columns containing multiple comma-separated values
def normalize_multi_value(series, corrections, sep=","):
    def clean_cell(x):
        if pd.isna(x):
            return x
        
        parts = [part.strip() for part in str(x).split(sep)]
        corrected_parts = [corrections.get(part.lower(), part) for part in parts]
        return ", ".join(corrected_parts)
    
    return series.apply(clean_cell)
In [ ]:
# -----------------------------
# Single-value columns
# -----------------------------
df_clean["employedAs"] = normalize_single_value(df_clean["employedAs"], employedAs_corrections)
df_clean["organizations"] = normalize_single_value(df_clean["organizations"], organizations_corrections)

df_clean["location"] = normalize_single_value(df_clean["location"], location_corrections)
df_clean["locality"] = normalize_single_value(df_clean["locality"], location_corrections)

df_clean["lengthOfSentence"] = normalize_single_value(df_clean["lengthOfSentence"], length_sentence_corrections)
df_clean["sentence"] = normalize_single_value(df_clean["sentence"], sentence_corrections)

# -----------------------------
# Multi-value columns
# -----------------------------
df_clean["jobs"] = normalize_multi_value(df_clean["jobs"], jobs_corrections)
df_clean["coverage"] = normalize_multi_value(df_clean["coverage"], coverage_corrections)
df_clean["mediums"] = normalize_multi_value(df_clean["mediums"], mediums_corrections)
df_clean["charges"] = normalize_multi_value(df_clean["charges"], charges_corrections)

Validation: Standardisation of categorical labels¶

In [ ]:
res = 0

for i, col in enumerate(cat_cols, start=1):
    variants = find_case_variants(df_clean, col)
    if not variants.empty:
        print(f"Column n°{i}: {col}")
        display(variants)
    else:
        res = res + 1

print("Nb columns valid:", res)

B) Correction of inconsistencies across columns¶

B.1) Multiple element in columns¶

The concerne columns as we saw in part 1, :

  • jobs, coverage, medium, charges, sourceOfFire (separate the data and create a other dataframe/table for the futur db)
  • location, locality, locationImprisoned (check the sens)
In [ ]:
# Cleaning of 'fullName' before creating the Id. 
df_clean["fullName"] = (
    df_clean["fullName"]
    .astype("string")
    .str.strip()
    .str.replace(r"\s+", " ", regex=True)
)

col = pd.factorize(df_clean["fullName"])[0] + 1
df_clean.insert(0, 'Id', col)

As we created new tables for the database (for the visualisation), we are going to create and correct the news dataframe created.

In [ ]:
# dataframe for job
df_jobs = (
    df_clean[["Id", "jobs"]]
    .dropna(subset=["jobs"])
    .assign(jobs=lambda x: x["jobs"].str.split(","))
    .explode("jobs")
)
df_jobs["jobs"] = df_jobs["jobs"].str.strip()
df_jobs = df_jobs.rename(columns={
    "Id": "J_Id",
    "jobs": "job"
})
df_jobs.reset_index(drop=True, inplace=True)

# dataframe for coverage
df_coverage = (
    df_clean[["Id", "coverage"]]
    .dropna(subset=["coverage"])
    .assign(coverage=lambda x: x["coverage"].str.split(","))
    .explode("coverage")
)
df_coverage["coverage"] = df_coverage["coverage"].str.strip()
df_coverage = df_coverage.rename(columns={
    "Id": "J_Id",
    "coverage": "coverage"
})

# datframe for medium
df_mediums = (
    df_clean[["Id", "mediums"]]
    .dropna(subset=["mediums"])
    .assign(mediums=lambda x: x["mediums"].str.split(","))
    .explode("mediums")
)

df_mediums["medium"] = df_mediums["mediums"].str.strip()
df_mediums = df_mediums.rename(columns={"Id": "J_Id"})
df_mediums = df_mediums[["J_Id", "medium"]]
df_mediums.reset_index(drop=True, inplace=True)

# dataframe for charges
df_charges = (
    df_clean[["Id", "charges"]]
    .dropna(subset=["charges"])
    .assign(charges=lambda x: x["charges"].str.split(","))
    .explode("charges")
)

df_charges["charge"] = df_charges["charges"].str.strip()
df_charges = df_charges.rename(columns={"Id": "J_Id"})
df_charges = df_charges[["J_Id", "charge"]]
df_charges.reset_index(drop=True, inplace=True)

# dataframe for sourceOfFire
df_fire = (
    df_clean[["Id", "sourcesOfFire"]]
    .dropna(subset=["sourcesOfFire"])
    .assign(sourcesOfFire=lambda x: x["sourcesOfFire"].str.split(","))
    .explode("sourcesOfFire")
)

df_fire["sourceOfFire"] = df_fire["sourcesOfFire"].str.strip()
df_fire = df_fire.rename(columns={"Id": "J_Id"})
df_fire = df_fire[["J_Id", "sourceOfFire"]]
df_fire.reset_index(drop=True, inplace=True)
In [ ]:
# Explication
df_jobs['job'] = df_jobs['job'].replace('Owner/Publisher', 'Publisher/Owner')
df_jobs['job'] = df_jobs['job'].replace('Columnist', 'Columnist/Commentator')
df_jobs = df_jobs[df_jobs["job"] != "Reporter"]
df_jobs.reset_index(drop=True, inplace=True)

df_coverage['coverage'] = df_coverage['coverage'].replace('Human Rights`', 'Human Rights')

df_charges['charge'] = df_charges['charge'].replace('Ethnic/Religious Insult', 'Ethnic or Religious Insult')
df_charges['charge'] = df_charges['charge'].replace('Religious or ethnic insult', 'Ethnic or Religious Insult')
df_charges = df_charges[df_charges["charge"].str.strip() != ""]
In [ ]:
# Explication
df_jobs = df_jobs.drop_duplicates(["J_Id", "job"])
df_coverage = df_coverage.drop_duplicates(["J_Id", "coverage"])
df_mediums = df_mediums.drop_duplicates(["J_Id", "medium"])
df_charges = df_charges.drop_duplicates(["J_Id", "charge"])
df_fire = df_fire.drop_duplicates(["J_Id", "sourceOfFire"])
df_jobs.reset_index(drop=True, inplace=True)
df_coverage.reset_index(drop=True, inplace=True)
df_mediums.reset_index(drop=True, inplace=True)
df_charges.reset_index(drop=True, inplace=True)
df_fire.reset_index(drop=True, inplace=True)

B.2) location, locality, locationImprisoned, country and region sub dataframe¶

In [ ]:
# Look if we keep location or locality as saw in the matrix
subset = df_clean.dropna(subset=["location", "locality"])
same = (subset["location"] == subset["locality"]).sum()
total = len(subset)

print("Same values:", same)
print("Total rows:", total)
print("Percentage:", round(same/total*100, 2), "%")

Same values: 2650
Total rows: 2650
Percentage: 100.0 %

We can conclude that the locality column does not provide additional information, since every row where it contains a value matches exactly the value found in the location column. To ensure no information is lost, the two columns are first merged by prioritizing values from location and using locality only where location is missing. After this consolidation, the redundant locality column is removed.

Information count unique
locality 2650 1119
location 2709 1134
In [ ]:
# Combine the two columns (prioritize location, fill missing values with locality)
df_clean["location"] = df_clean["location"].fillna(df_clean["locality"])

# Remove the redundant column
df_clean = df_clean.drop(columns=["locality"])

# Verification after fusion
df_clean["location"].isna().sum()
Out[ ]:
4054

Now that we have the right list of columns for indentify the locatisation, we are going to recreate a hierachie following this order :

Champ Country Region Location Location of Imprisoned
Meaning Country where the event happened. Administrative region (province / state). City or precise location. Prison or facility where the journalist was held.

B.3) Geographical Consistency Cases¶

1. Country + Region/no Region + no Location + no LocationOfImprisonment¶

Action : Keep as is.

Rationale : Geographic information is already available at country and region level.

2. Country + Region + Location + no LocationOfImprisonment¶

Action : Keep as is.

Rationale : Missing locationOfImprisonment should not be replaced, as this field is only relevant for imprisonment cases.

3. Country + no Region + Location + no LocationOfImprisonment¶

Action : Conditional Update.

Rationale : Where possible, derive region only if it is explicitly contained in the location field. Otherwise, leave it missing.

4. Country + no Region + no Location + LocationOfImprisonment¶

Action : Manual Review.

Rationale : Review case by case to determine whether the imprisonment location contains usable information for region or location.

5. Country + no Region + no Location + no LocationOfImprisonment¶

Action : Keep as is.

Rationale : No additional geographic information is available.

In [ ]:
geo_df = df_clean[["Id", "country", "region", "location", "locationImprisoned"]].copy()

    df_clean["country"].notna() &
    df_clean["region"].isna() &
    df_clean["location"].notna() &
    df_clean["locationImprisoned"].isna()

Due to the large number of cases (2008 rows), missing regions were not inferred automatically to avoid introducing potential geographic inaccuracies.

In [ ]:
# Mask for rows with a comma in location
mask_comma = case3["location"].str.contains(",", na=False)

# Extract city (before comma)
df_clean.loc[case3.index[mask_comma], "location"] = (
    case3.loc[mask_comma, "location"]
    .str.split(",", n=1)
    .str[0]
    .str.strip()
)

# Extract region (after comma)
df_clean.loc[case3.index[mask_comma], "region"] = (
    case3.loc[mask_comma, "location"]
    .str.split(",", n=1)
    .str[1]
    .str.strip()
)
In [ ]:

    df_clean["country"].notna() &
    df_clean["region"].isna() &
    df_clean["location"].isna() &
    df_clean["locationImprisoned"].notna()
].copy()

B.4) Small Inconsitancy (Gender/Location & Organisation/Other)¶

In [ ]:
def clean_location_row_simple(row):
    loc = row["location"]
    reg = row["region"]

    if pd.isna(loc):
        return pd.Series([loc, reg])

    loc = str(loc).strip().rstrip(",").replace("Xi‚Äôan", "Xi'an")

    if "," not in loc:
        return pd.Series([loc, reg])

    left, right = [p.strip() for p in loc.split(",", 1)]

    if pd.isna(reg):
        reg = right

    return pd.Series([left, reg])

df_clean[["location", "region"]] = df_clean.apply(clean_location_row_simple, axis=1)
In [ ]:
prison_keywords = "prison|jail|police station"

mask = df_clean["location"].str.contains(prison_keywords, case=False, na=False)

df_clean.loc[mask, "locationImprisoned"] = df_clean.loc[mask, "location"]
df_clean.loc[mask, "location"] = np.nan
In [ ]:
same_region_location = df_clean[
    df_clean["region"].notna() &
    df_clean["location"].notna() &
    (df_clean["region"] == df_clean["location"])
][["Id", "country", "region", "location"]]

display(same_region_location)

396 rows × 4 columns

In some cases, the location of arrest and the location of imprisonment appear identical. Since the dataset does not explicitly distinguish whether imprisonment occurred in a specific facility or within the same city, no modification was made to avoid introducing assumptions not supported by the data.

In [ ]:
same_location_imprisoned = df_clean[
    df_clean["location"].notna() &
    df_clean["locationImprisoned"].notna() &
    (df_clean["location"] == df_clean["locationImprisoned"])
][["Id", "country", "region", "location", "locationImprisoned"]]

display(same_location_imprisoned)

In [ ]:
u_table = pd.DataFrame({"n_unique": df_clean.nunique(dropna=True)})
u_table["ttl_size"] = len(df_clean)
u_table["unique_%"] = (u_table["n_unique"] / len(df_clean) * 100).round(2)
u_table["NaN_%"] = (df_clean.isna().mean() * 100).round(2)

print("------------------------------------------------")
print("Column Resume: Unique Values and Missing Data")
print("------------------------------------------------")
display(u_table.sort_values("n_unique", ascending=False))

In [ ]:
# We are going to remove some columns like province, state 
df_clean["region"] = df_clean["region"].fillna(df_clean["province"])
df_clean["region"] = df_clean["region"].fillna(df_clean["state"])
df_clean = df_clean.drop(columns=['state', 'province', 'impCountry', 'type', 'lengthOfSentence'])
df_clean.columns
Out[ ]:
Index(['Id', 'year', 'fullName', 'primaryNationality', 'gender', 'photoUrl',
       'typeOfDeath', 'status', 'employedAs', 'organizations',
       'localOrForeign', 'motiveConfirmed', 'impunityMurder', 'tortured',
       'captive', 'threatened', 'sentence', 'country', 'region', 'location',
       'locationImprisoned'],
      dtype='object')

C) Global Validation¶

In [ ]:
columns = df_clean.select_dtypes(include="object").columns
matrix = pd.DataFrame(index=columns, columns=columns, dtype=float)

for col1 in columns:
    set1 = set(df_clean[col1].dropna().astype(str))

    for col2 in columns:
        set2 = set(df_clean[col2].dropna().astype(str))

        intersection = len(set1 & set2)
        union = len(set1 | set2)

        if union == 0:
            score = 0
        else:
            score = intersection / union

        matrix.loc[col1, col2] = score

plt.figure(figsize=(24,10))

mask = (matrix == 0)

sns.heatmap(
    matrix,
    mask=mask,
    cmap="YlOrBr",
    annot=True,
    fmt=".3%",
    vmax=0.1,
    norm=LogNorm(vmin=0.0001, vmax=1),
    linewidths=1,
    cbar=False
)

plt.title("Column Similarity Matrix", fontsize=16)
plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha="right")
plt.yticks(rotation=0)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
